source.seedLabel: Roee Hartuv on pricing & packaging in the AI era

topics.title: Pricing Is The Magic Growth Lever Nobody Owns
topics.angle: Most B2B SaaS companies don't hire a dedicated pricing leader until $500M ARR. That's why pricing is broken at every earlier stage — it sits in nobody's lane: half product, half CFO, half CRO, fully orphaned.
topics.sourceQuote: There are not a lot of experts out there, or companies don't have a pricing and packaging expert until they reach that 500 million ARR. Before that, like sometimes it's a CRO, sometimes the CFO, sometimes it's product or product marketing.

topics.title: Why Pricing Beats Every Other GTM Lever
topics.angle: Pricing changes compound across acquisition, onboarding, renewal, and expansion in weeks. Sales coaching, enablement, and process work take quarters — and unwind the moment a key human leaves. Fix pricing, you fix the system.
topics.sourceQuote: Most of the other levers take time to see results... you can train and teach and introduce tools, but as soon as somebody leaves the organization, then it's back to square one... if we fix pricing and packaging, that unlocks many different opportunities along the customer journey.

topics.title: The Packaging Order Of Operations
topics.angle: Most teams start pricing conversations with 'what number do we charge.' That's backwards. The non-negotiable sequence: packaging first, then pricing structure, then pricing metric, THEN the number. Skipping steps is why your pricing meetings go in circles.
topics.sourceQuote: We start off with packaging and then we do the pricing structure and then the pricing metric. The pricing metric is what we price on, and then how do we price?... And the price point comes last.

topics.title: Feature-Rank Packaging Is Dead
topics.angle: The old way: rank features by usage, dump the top 60% in Basic, next 30% in Pro, last 10% in Premium. That packaging is the reason your sellers can't actually value-sell — the bundles don't map to anything a buyer is trying to accomplish.
topics.sourceQuote: The classical way of doing packaging... let's rank all the features and how many customers are using it. And let's take the first 60%... put that under the basic. The better package gets the 60 and additional 30. And then the best is that 10% of features.

topics.title: You Can't Value-Sell If Your Packaging Isn't Built On Value
topics.angle: Every B2B team claims to do value selling. Almost none actually do — because their packages are organized around features, not jobs to be done. The seller can talk value all day, but the price sheet drags every conversation back to feature comparison.
topics.sourceQuote: Every time, like we do value selling, of course, everybody wants to do value selling. But only when I got into pricing and packaging, I discovered that you can talk and you can say that you're doing value selling, but if your packaging is not aligned to values and jobs to be done, then you can talk all you want.

topics.title: The $12K vs $13K Myth
topics.angle: In B2B, the difference between $12K and $13K doesn't decide the deal. Sellers obsess over the number because they don't have a framework for the actual drivers — packaging, structure, and metric. The price point is the LEAST important lever you have.
topics.sourceQuote: It doesn't matter for our customer if it's 12,000 or 13,000. The deal will not we won't win or lose. It's not like retail or B2C... we all know that if we put our best sellers, they will be able to sell at 12 and 13 or even 15. Because it's all about value.

topics.title: The Complexity Budget
topics.angle: Every deal has a 'complexity budget' — the amount of intricacy your buyer will tolerate in pricing before they walk. AWS enterprise contracts can absorb dozens of line items. A $10K/year startup deal can absorb almost none. Match pricing complexity to ACV or you'll lose the bottom of your market.
topics.sourceQuote: We have a budget of complexity. Now obviously the higher your ACV... the bigger they are, the more complexity you can add into the sales process or your pricing... when I'm selling to a startup that is just starting off and the ACV there is very low, the complexity has to be very low as well.

topics.title: Classic SaaS Margins Were A Historical Anomaly
topics.angle: 80-95% gross margins were a once-in-a-generation gift. SaaS only worked that way because marginal cost to serve was effectively zero. AI breaks the math — and most SaaS founders are running the old playbook on the new economics without realizing it yet.
topics.sourceQuote: In SaaS, our cost to serve is almost zero. The marginal cost to serve. The tenth client or the ten thousandth client is the same... That's why our margins were so high. Our margins was in classic SaaS eighty, ninety, ninety-five percent... But we all know that AI comes with a cost.

topics.title: Even OpenAI Loses Money On Your Subscription
topics.angle: OpenAI's published financials confirm it: at $20/month, they're underwater on heavy users. If the world's most-funded AI company can't make seat pricing work for AI, your seat-based SaaS+AI bolt-on isn't working either. You just haven't been measuring.
topics.sourceQuote: Even the best company in the world... they're losing money. So even them, they are losing money. So all the rest of us probably doing not as good as they are... Coming back to OpenAI, Claude. I'm using 20, I pay twenty dollars a month. I'm sure that I'm costing them five times that.

topics.title: $40M Bootstrap To Bleeding In 90 Days
topics.angle: A profitable, bootstrapped, $40M ARR category leader launched AI features in November on their existing seat-based plan. By February they were hemorrhaging money. The lesson: bolting AI onto seat pricing is the single fastest way to destroy a healthy SaaS P&L.
topics.sourceQuote: I recently did a work for a company that they bootstrap forty million in ARR. They're probably the leader in their space, and they introduced AI into a seat-based model. They launched that in November of last year. In February they called us up and told us, hey, we were a profitable bootstrap company. Since we did this launch, we're losing money like crazy.

topics.title: SaaS Companies Don't Have The Tools To Measure Cost-To-Serve
topics.angle: Ask a SaaS-native company for unit economics by customer and you'll get blank stares. They've never had to measure it — marginal cost was zero. Now AI makes cost-to-serve material, and most product and finance stacks have no way to even report on it.
topics.sourceQuote: In SAS, we only cared about top line... All of a sudden we're introducing another line item which is cost to serve. AI native SaaS... they don't even have the tools to measure that... what is the unit economics of selling to this type of client? We don't know. They don't even have the infrastructure to measure that.

topics.title: Outcome-Based Pricing Is Mostly A Trap
topics.angle: Three talks at a recent London RevOps event all pitched outcome-based pricing as the future for everyone. It's not. It works for narrow vertical AI companies with a single, agreeable outcome. For horizontal tools, it's the buzzword that's going to wreck your P&L.
topics.sourceQuote: I was at a RevOps event in London. There were three presentations on outcome-based pricing. And it's usually framed as everyone should do it... outcome based is different when you're selling to a horizontal solution or horizontal segment. Different use cases, outcome means different things for different users.

topics.title: The 3 Questions To Decide If Outcome-Based Pricing Works
topics.angle: Before you copy Fin AI's closed-ticket model, run the three-question gate: (1) Can you clearly define ONE outcome? (2) Will your customers agree with that definition? (3) Is the value of that outcome the same across your customer base? Three yeses, and you're a candidate. Anything less, stay on usage.
topics.sourceQuote: Can you ask what is the outcome and you can clearly define that... Can your customers define the same outcome? And can you agree with your customers on that outcome?... And then the third question is the value similar to all your different customers?

topics.title: Show Me One Good Outcome-Based Pricing Example
topics.angle: Everyone in the agentic AI conversation says 'price on outcomes.' Almost nobody can name three companies actually doing it well outside of Fin AI. The vocabulary is racing way ahead of the playbook.
topics.sourceQuote: I haven't seen a lot of good examples of outcome based pricing out there. Everybody wants to say, Hey, when you introduce a Gentic AI, you should price based on on outcomes. Great, show me a company that does it well. I haven't seen.

topics.title: Customers Hate Usage Pricing — Here's How To Fix It
topics.angle: CFOs don't fear usage-based pricing because it's expensive. They fear it because it's unpredictable. Bill shock kills renewals. The fix is mechanical: base fee + usage on top, real-time visibility, IT-level controls per team, and audit-grade traceability per token.
topics.sourceQuote: Customers hate to buy usage based products... it's not predictable... CFOs really see scared... bill shock is a real term. One of the best mechanisms is to create some sort of recurring base fee and usage on top of that... you need to create visibility to your customers. Do they know how much they're using in real time?

topics.title: Your First Big Usage Bill Triggers The Audit
topics.angle: Founders launching usage-based pricing always underestimate the operational tail. The first time a customer gets a five-figure bill they didn't expect, they'll demand a token-level audit. If you can't trace every dollar back to a query, by user, by hour — your renewal is in trouble before it starts.
topics.sourceQuote: I think the first time you send out a massive bill to a customer, they're gonna demand the audit anyway. And then you're gonna have to go lay down all of that infrastructure to make sure you can report against it.

topics.title: The Jobs-To-Be-Done Validation Test
topics.angle: Real packaging validation: put your three tiers in front of a customer with the jobs-to-be-done labeled on each. They should be able to point to ONE without hesitation. If they say 'I'm a little of this and a little of that,' your packaging just failed.
topics.sourceQuote: I present the different packaging and say, hey, these are the jobs to be done. Tier number one, this is what it allows you to do... Can you find yourself which jobs to be done describes you the most? And if they're able to pick without any hesitation, that's the best result that we can find. But if they're saying, I don't know, this is me, but that's also me... that's where we fail.

topics.title: The Reverse-Risk Pricing Rollout
topics.angle: Don't migrate your biggest customer to new pricing first. Reverse the order: design with insiders, validate with sales leadership, test with new business in your least-important region, then your second-tier customers, and ONLY THEN your most important account. End state first, sequence backwards from there.
topics.sourceQuote: The end goal is to introduce the new pricing and packaging and migrate our most important customer... So in order to move our most important client, before we do that, we want to test it out with maybe the second, third, and fourth and fifth most important customers... we test and iterate and build confidence as we go.

topics.title: Pricing Should Be A Product, Not A Tablet From The Mountain
topics.angle: The old rule was 'set pricing, don't touch it for five years.' Dead. If your product ships new value monthly, your pricing should evolve at the same cadence. Treat pricing like a product team — iterations, releases, customer feedback loops.
topics.sourceQuote: Gone are the days that you said pricing and packaging, and like when you build a company and then don't visit it for five to seven years... we have reached a point where pricing and packaging should be referred to as product. Like we keep on improving, adapting.

topics.title: Revisit Pricing On Whichever Cycle Is Longer
topics.angle: Concrete cadence: revisit pricing either every product release cycle OR every sales cycle, whichever is longer. Six-month enterprise cycle? Pricing review every six months. Weekly product releases? Defer to the sales cadence. Stop letting price decay quietly.
topics.sourceQuote: I would say revisit your pricing and packaging either every product release cycle or sales cycle? Whatever is takes longer. So if I'm selling six months of sales cycle, every six months I would say, okay, let's look at the last six months and let's see this cohort.

topics.title: Anthropic's Weekly Pricing Experiments
topics.angle: Pay attention to what Claude is doing. Every week: a new packaging tweak, a new credit promo, a new bundle. They're not 'changing pricing' — they're treating pricing as a feature surface that ships weekly because the product does too. That's the new bar.
topics.sourceQuote: I'm a Claude user... if you are, you probably notice every week they're changing something about their pricing and packaging... they keep on changing and iterating. And they know why? Because they're launching new features in Claude on a weekly basis.

topics.title: Pricing Cost Me More Sleep Than Sales, Hiring, Or Delivery
topics.angle: Five years building a services firm, and pricing was the hardest problem of all of them. Messaging, sales motion, delivery, recruiting — those had paths forward. Pricing and packaging didn't. If you're a founder feeling stuck on it, you're not alone — and it's not because you're bad at business.
topics.sourceQuote: I started the company in 2021 and pricing and packaging has been and continues to be one of the biggest challenges that I have had in our business. It hasn't been the messaging, it hasn't been the sales, it hasn't been the delivery, it hasn't been the recruiting... I personally have had a really, really tough time with this topic.

topics.title: Why The Customer Workshop Is Where Packaging Lives Or Dies
topics.angle: Packaging workshops fail when the team can't stop staring at the feature list. The skill is dragging the room from 'here's what we built' to 'here's what the customer is trying to achieve.' Your packaging session needs a forcing function to keep the conversation on the buyer.
topics.sourceQuote: I had a workshop yesterday with a new client... I did not do a good job. I wasn't able to take them from the mindset of hey, this is the product and this is the features... I wanted them to look at the customer and let's think of what the customer is trying to achieve.

topics.title: Your Biggest Customer Might Belong In Your Cheapest Tier
topics.angle: Counterintuitive truth from real packaging work: a top-3 retail logo might only use one basic feature. That doesn't mean discount them — it means your entry package is where they belong, and your job is to build the staircase of value that pulls them upward over time.
topics.sourceQuote: One of the biggest retail companies is their customers and they just use one basic functionality of the product... that's our first package. That is our entry package... let's build packages that grow. So we're hoping that we can take them from just using that basic feature into more advanced features as we move along.

topics.title: Outcome vs Consumption Are Two Different Questions
topics.angle: Stop conflating them. Outcome-based pricing is WHAT you charge for. Consumption-based pricing is HOW you meter. You can stack them — Fin AI charges by closed ticket (outcome) consumed (usage). Understanding the difference fixes half the bad pricing conversations on LinkedIn.
topics.sourceQuote: Outcome-based pricing is the what you price on. You price on outcomes. So that's one topic. Usage based is how... And you could do both, right? I could do consumption based on outcome.

topics.title: Multi-Segment Companies Need Multi-Complexity Pricing
topics.angle: If you sell to startups AND enterprise, you don't need one elegant pricing page — you need two architectures running in parallel. Startup pricing absorbs almost zero complexity. Enterprise pricing rewards it. Trying to make one model serve both is why your sales team can't quote either segment cleanly.
topics.sourceQuote: If you're selling to multiple segments and multiple types of customers, you might have multiple levels of complexity. That's also where packaging might come into the play as well.

topics.title: Pricing Is An Operations And Product Problem, Not A Sales Problem
topics.angle: When CROs own pricing, they tune for sellability and miss cost-to-serve. When product owns it, they build feature ladders and miss value capture. AI pricing requires product to ship metering, ops to ship traceability, and revenue to ship the offer in lockstep. Stop putting pricing in one team's lane.
topics.sourceQuote: So every company that now wants to introduce AI needs to understand that it's not only a pricing and packaging exercise. It's an operational and a product exercise as well.

posts.author: Anthony Enrico
posts.topicTitle: The Packaging Order Of Operations
posts.postText:
**Most teams start pricing conversations with "what number do we charge."**

That's backwards. And it's why your pricing meetings go in circles.

The number comes LAST. There's a sequence — and skipping a step downstream is what creates the chaos upstream.

We put together a quick visual on the non-negotiable order of operations we use with every B2B SaaS client we touch.

Swipe through 👇
posts.voiceNotes: Bolded thesis hook in his classic 'most teams are doing X — that's backwards' construction. Em-dash asides, short punchy paragraphs, signature 'Swipe through' + pointing-down emoji CTA that maps to his observed carousel close pattern.
posts.visualAssetNeeded: A 5-slide carousel walking through the packaging-then-pricing sequence: cover slide framing the contrarian thesis, four framework slides numbered 01/02/03/04 for Packaging, Pricing Structure, Pricing Metric, and Price Point, and a closing dark slide with the payoff line about why teams skip steps.

params.title: The Packaging Order Of Operations
params.body: Four decisions, in order. Skip one and your pricing meetings go in circles.

params.title: Packaging
params.body: What's in the box? Decide what you're selling before you decide how to sell it. Good/Better/Best, modules, tiers — this is the architecture.

params.title: Pricing Structure
params.body: Subscription? Usage? Hybrid? Outcome-based? The structure decides how your customer experiences cost over time.

params.title: Pricing Metric
params.body: What you price ON. Seats, API calls, GB processed, agents deployed. Pick the metric that scales with the value your buyer feels.

params.title: The Number
params.body: Now — and only now — you set the price point. The number is the easy part once the first three are locked.

params.title: Skip a step. Pay later.
params.body: Most teams jump straight to the number and reverse-engineer the rest. That's why pricing feels broken. Run the sequence.

posts.author: Anthony Enrico
posts.topicTitle: The Complexity Budget
posts.postText:
**Every deal has a "complexity budget."**

It's the amount of intricacy your buyer will tolerate in your pricing before they walk away.

An AWS enterprise contract can absorb dozens of line items, usage tiers, and commitments. A $10K/year startup deal can absorb almost none.

The rule we use with B2B SaaS clients: match pricing complexity to ACV. Higher ACV = more sophisticated buyer = more complexity allowed. Lower ACV = self-serve buyer = ruthless simplicity required.

Founders blow this all the time at the bottom of the market. They design enterprise-grade pricing matrices for SMB deals — and watch their conversion rate fall off a cliff.

One image, the whole concept 👇
posts.voiceNotes: Bolded contrarian-truth hook, em-dash asides, plain operator vocabulary (ACV, conversion, SMB), specific dollar figures, and a clean payoff line. Single CTA emoji at the end — not signature-stamped.
posts.visualAssetNeeded: A single 1080x1080 stat-style infographic with the headline 'The Complexity Budget,' a big visual showing ACV on one axis and tolerable complexity on the other, and a one-line rule at the bottom about matching one to the other.

params.title: The Complexity Budget
params.body: Every deal has a budget of complexity your buyer will tolerate. Enterprise contracts can absorb dozens of line items. A $10K startup deal can absorb almost none. Match pricing complexity to ACV — or lose the bottom of your market.

posts.author: Anthony Enrico
posts.topicTitle: Pricing Cost Me More Sleep Than Sales, Hiring, Or Delivery
posts.postText:
I started LeanScale in 2021.

In five years, pricing and packaging has been the single hardest problem I've worked on.

Not messaging. Not the sales motion. Not delivery. Not recruiting. Those had paths forward — frameworks I'd seen work, operators I could learn from, playbooks I could borrow.

Pricing didn't.

It sits between product, finance, sales, and the customer's psychology — and the right answer changes every time the market does. There's no SOP that survives the next ICP shift, the next competitor move, the next AI-shaped change to the cost structure.

If you're a founder feeling stuck on this one — you're not alone. And it's not because you're bad at business. It's because pricing is genuinely the hardest puzzle in B2B, and most of us are figuring it out in public.

The only thing that's worked for me: stop chasing the perfect answer, start running the sequence (packaging → structure → metric → price), and rebuild the model every 6-12 months.

You don't solve pricing. You operate it.
posts.voiceNotes: Personal story but grounded — no vulnerability bait, no 'I almost quit' arc (which his profile flags as avoidance). Honest founder-peer register, opens with company-founding date matching his 'I started the company in 2021' phrasing. Lands on a tactical aphorism ('You don't solve pricing. You operate it.') instead of a CTA — varies the close.

posts.author: Anthony Enrico
posts.topicTitle: $40M Bootstrap To Bleeding In 90 Days
posts.postText:
We recently did work for a $40M ARR, bootstrapped, profitable category leader.

They launched AI features in November. By February they called us.

"We were profitable for years. Since the AI launch, we're losing money like crazy."

Here's what happened: they bolted AI onto their existing seat-based plan. Same pricing model, new feature set. Customers loved it. Adoption was through the roof.

That was the problem.

Every additional user on the AI feature dragged compute cost up linearly. Seat pricing didn't move. They were selling $40 worth of inference for the price of a $20 seat — at scale — and the gross margin curve collapsed in 90 days.

This is the trap most B2B SaaS leaders are walking into right now:

— Seat pricing was built for low-marginal-cost software
— AI features have real per-use COGS
— Bolting one onto the other guarantees a P&L blowup

If you're shipping AI on top of a seat-based model, the question isn't "how do we capture more value." It's "how do we stop bleeding the value we already had."

Fix the pricing structure before the board meeting where the gross margin chart shows up.
posts.voiceNotes: Classic case-study opener ('We recently did work for...') with concrete numbers, anonymized customer, mini-dialogue quote, and operator vocabulary (COGS, gross margin, seat pricing). Uses em-dash bullets like his observed style. Closes with a tactical imperative instead of a CTA emoji — anti-repetition.

posts.author: Anthony Enrico
posts.topicTitle: Pricing Is The Magic Growth Lever Nobody Owns
posts.postText:
**Most B2B SaaS companies don't hire a dedicated pricing leader until $500M ARR.**

That's why pricing is broken at every earlier stage.

It sits in nobody's lane. Half product. Half CFO. Half CRO. Fully orphaned.

The biggest growth lever in the entire P&L — the one with more leverage on EBITDA than any hire or campaign — gets owned by committee until the company is too big to fix it without scar tissue.

The fix isn't a $300K head of pricing at Series A. It's deciding NOW who has the pen on packaging, structure, metric, and the number — and revisiting it every two quarters with real data.

Ownership beats expertise. Always has.
posts.voiceNotes: Bolded contrarian-truth hook with a hard statistic, his 'most companies are doing X — here's why' construction, and a clean payoff line ('Ownership beats expertise. Always has.') with no emoji and no CTA. Variation: shortest post in the batch, no sign-off stamp.

posts.author: Joe Zaghloul
posts.topicTitle: Customers Hate Usage Pricing — Here's How To Fix It
posts.postText:
We've been deep in pricing & packaging conversations with our AI-native B2B customers for months now.

One pattern keeps surfacing: CFOs don't push back on usage-based pricing because it's expensive. They push back because it's unpredictable.

Bill shock is killing renewals. The fix is mechanical, not philosophical — base fee + usage on top, real-time visibility, team-level controls, traceability per consumption unit.

Swipe through the build order we're recommending 👇

#revops #pricing
posts.voiceNotes: Opens with 'We've been deep in' — pattern-recognition framing from the operator vantage point he's earned. Tight paragraphs, RevOps shorthand (CFOs, renewals, AI-native), 👇 link-pointer, hashtag bundle close.
posts.visualAssetNeeded: 5-slide carousel on de-risking usage-based pricing for B2B AI buyers. Opens with a cover slide naming the problem (CFOs fear unpredictability, not cost). Middle slides walk the four-part fix: base fee + usage layer, real-time consumption visibility, IT-level team controls, audit-grade traceability per unit. Closes with a dark-template summary slide on what predictable usage pricing actually looks like for the buyer.

params.title: Customers Hate Usage Pricing. Here's How To Fix It.
params.body: CFOs don't fear usage-based pricing because it's expensive. They fear it because it's unpredictable. Bill shock kills renewals.

params.title: Base Fee + Usage On Top
params.body: Anchor the contract with a recurring floor. Layer usage above it. CFOs can forecast the floor and only sweat the variable layer.

params.title: Real-Time Consumption Visibility
params.body: Buyers should never wait for an invoice to know what they spent. Show usage live, by team, by user, by unit.

params.title: IT-Level Controls Per Team
params.body: Caps, alerts, and team-level budgets. Give IT the same governance posture they expect from any cloud spend tool.

params.title: Audit-Grade Traceability
params.body: Every consumption unit tied to a user, prompt, agent run, or workflow. When finance asks 'what drove this spike?' the answer is one click away.

params.title: Predictability Sells. Surprises Don't.
params.body: Usage pricing isn't the problem. The lack of a floor, visibility, controls, and traceability is. Ship those four and renewals stop being a fight.

posts.author: Joe Zaghloul
posts.topicTitle: Revisit Pricing On Whichever Cycle Is Longer
posts.postText:
Most B2B startups we work with let pricing decay quietly.

The cleanest cadence we've seen: revisit pricing & packaging every product release cycle OR every sales cycle — whichever is longer.

Six-month enterprise sales cycle? Pricing review every six months. Weekly product releases? Defer to the sales cadence so you're reviewing real cohort behavior, not noise.

Simple rule. Hard to argue with. One image below to make it stick.

#revops #pricing
posts.voiceNotes: Operator-to-operator framing — names the pattern, gives the rule, anchors it with concrete numbers (six months, weekly). 'Simple rule. Hard to argue with.' uses his short-fragment cadence sparingly. Hashtag bundle close, no salute emoji this time.
posts.visualAssetNeeded: Single-slide infographic showing the pricing review cadence rule. Headline states the rule: revisit pricing on whichever cycle is longer — product release or sales cycle. A simple visual contrast: a six-month sales cycle bar vs. a weekly product release bar, with an arrow showing 'longer cycle wins.' A concrete example callout: 6-month sales cycle = pricing review every 6 months.

params.title: Revisit Pricing On Whichever Cycle Is Longer
params.body: Review pricing & packaging every product release OR every sales cycle — whichever takes longer. 6-month sales cycle? Review every 6 months. Weekly releases? Defer to the sales cadence so you're reading real cohort behavior, not noise.

posts.author: Joe Zaghloul
posts.topicTitle: SaaS Companies Don't Have The Tools To Measure Cost-To-Serve
posts.postText:
Pattern we're seeing across the AI-native B2B startups we work with:

Ask a SaaS-native team for unit economics by customer and you get blank stares.

They've never had to measure cost-to-serve. Marginal cost was effectively zero. Their product analytics, their billing stack, their finance close — none of it was built to report on it.

Now AI makes cost-to-serve material. A heavy-usage customer can be a margin drag, not a logo win. And the tooling gap is real — most teams literally cannot answer 'what's the unit economics on this segment?'

The operators getting ahead of it are wiring cost-per-call, cost-per-agent-run, and cost-per-workflow into the same dashboard as ARR and NRR. Right now. Not next planning cycle.
posts.voiceNotes: Classic Joe pattern-recognition opener ('Pattern we're seeing across...') lifted from his sample phrasings. Operator vantage point earned by 50+ implementations. Lands with a 'right now, not next cycle' urgency that matches his AI-stack-today posture.

posts.author: Joe Zaghloul
posts.topicTitle: You Can't Value-Sell If Your Packaging Isn't Built On Value
posts.postText:
Every B2B team we talk to says they do value selling.

Almost none actually do — and the reason isn't the sellers.

It's the packaging. If your packages are organized around features (Starter / Pro / Enterprise with a feature checklist), the price sheet drags every conversation back to feature comparison. The seller can talk value all day. The buyer is still mentally diffing your matrix against the competitor's.

Value selling requires packages built around jobs to be done, outcomes, and the value driver the buyer actually cares about. That's a packaging problem, not a training problem.

Fix the package. The sales motion follows.
posts.voiceNotes: Soft educational explainer — names the confusion ('it's not the sellers'), gives the mental model (packaging organizes the conversation), lands with a clean rule. Tight 3-4 sentence paragraphs, no emoji, no hashtag — varies the close.

posts.author: Joe Zaghloul
posts.topicTitle: The $12K vs $13K Myth
posts.postText:
The price point is the least important lever you have.

In B2B, the difference between $12K and $13K doesn't decide the deal.

Sellers obsess over the number because they don't have a framework for the actual drivers — packaging, structure, and metric. Drop your best seller into the same deal at $12K, $13K, or $15K and they'll close all three. Because they're selling value, not negotiating around a number.

If your team is fighting over price points, the fight you're actually losing is upstream — in how the offer is packaged and what it's priced on.

🫡
posts.voiceNotes: Slightly sharper for Joe — but the contrarian claim is backed with specifics (the $12/$13/$15K example, the three real drivers) and lands on packaging, which is his domain. Uses the 🫡 salute close once in the batch to honor his signature without over-using it.

posts.author: Cameron Legge
posts.topicTitle: The Reverse-Risk Pricing Rollout
posts.postText:
Pricing and packaging changes are scary because most teams roll them out in the wrong order.

We usually start with the customer who matters most. That's where the risk is highest.

The move is to flip it. Design the end state first, then sequence the rollout backwards from there — insiders, sales leadership, new business in a small region, second-tier accounts, and your most important customer last.

By the time you get to the account that matters most, you have months of evidence behind you.

Swipe through the sequence we use with clients.
posts.voiceNotes: Teacherly downshift register he uses for tactical playbooks. No contrarian framing — frames the lesson as 'the move is to flip it' rather than 'everyone is wrong.' Short connective prose, payoff line, no sales CTA.
posts.visualAssetNeeded: 5-slide carousel walking through the reverse-order pricing rollout sequence — open with cover, then 4 numbered framework slides for each rollout stage, closing on a dark slide with the payoff line.

params.title: Start at the end. Sequence backwards.
params.body: The order you roll out new pricing matters as much as the pricing itself.

params.title: Design with insiders first
params.body: Pull your CS and product leads into the room. Pressure-test the model before any customer sees it.

params.title: Validate with sales leadership
params.body: Get your CRO and frontline leaders behind it. If they can't sell it internally, they won't sell it externally.

params.title: Test on new business in your smallest region
params.body: Lowest stakes, fastest feedback. You learn the objections without exposing real revenue.

params.title: Migrate your second tier next
params.body: Customers 2, 3, 4, and 5. Build the migration muscle before the one that matters most.

params.title: Then, and only then, your top account.
params.body: By the time you get there, you have months of evidence and a tested motion. That's how you protect the customer you can't afford to lose.

posts.author: Cameron Legge
posts.topicTitle: Outcome vs Consumption Are Two Different Questions
posts.postText:
Half the pricing debates on LinkedIn are people arguing about two different things.

Outcome-based pricing is WHAT you charge for. The customer pays when something good happens — a ticket gets resolved, a deal gets booked, a meeting gets set.

Consumption-based pricing is HOW you meter. Per query, per seat, per minute.

These are not competing models. You can stack them. Fin AI charges by closed ticket — that's the outcome — consumed across the month — that's the consumption.

Get the two questions separated and a lot of the noise clears up. Cheers!
posts.voiceNotes: Soft educational explainer in his teacherly register. Uses a real example (Fin AI) for concreteness. Closes with 'Cheers!' — his signature closer, used once in this batch.
posts.visualAssetNeeded: Single infographic with a two-column comparison: left column 'Outcome-Based (the WHAT)' with examples, right column 'Consumption-Based (the HOW)' with examples, and a bottom bar showing they can stack — with Fin AI as the worked example.

params.title: Outcome vs consumption are two different questions.
items: Outcome-based pricing is WHAT you charge for — resolved tickets, booked meetings, closed deals.
Consumption-based pricing is HOW you meter — per query, per seat, per minute, per token.
They are not competing models. You can stack them.
Fin AI charges by closed ticket (outcome) consumed across the month (consumption).
Separate the two questions and most pricing debates get a lot shorter.

posts.author: Cameron Legge
posts.topicTitle: Your First Big Usage Bill Triggers The Audit
posts.postText:
Pattern I'm seeing with founders rolling out usage-based pricing.

They underestimate the operational tail.

The first time a customer gets a five-figure bill they didn't see coming, they will demand a token-level audit. Every dollar, traced back to a query, by user, by hour.

If you can't produce it, the renewal conversation is in trouble before it even starts.

The metering, the reporting, the customer-facing dashboard — all of that infrastructure has to exist BEFORE the first surprise bill, not after. Build it once on your timeline or build it twice on theirs.
posts.voiceNotes: Pattern-recognition voice — uses 'Pattern I'm seeing' opener consistent with the INDUSTRY OBSERVATION template. Calm, instructive teacherly downshift. No 'Cheers!' or signature close — clean payoff line ('Build it once on your timeline or build it twice on theirs').

posts.author: Cameron Legge
posts.topicTitle: Why The Customer Workshop Is Where Packaging Lives Or Dies
posts.postText:
Ran a packaging workshop with a new client yesterday. Honestly, I didn't do a great job.

The whole session, the team kept pulling the conversation back to the product. Here's what we built. Here's the feature list. Here's the roadmap.

I wanted them to flip it. Look at the customer. What is the customer actually trying to achieve? What outcome are they buying? What does success look like a year from now?

Every time we got close, someone would surface a feature and we'd be right back where we started.

The lesson I took home: the workshop needs a forcing function. A prompt taped to the wall, a question on every slide, something that drags the room back to the buyer every time it drifts.

Packaging conversations don't fail on smart people. They fail when there's nothing in the room loud enough to keep the customer at the center.

Going back next week with a better structure.
posts.voiceNotes: Personal story / vulnerability done in his voice — sincere, not performative, owns the mistake without self-flagellation. Quiet landing line ('Going back next week with a better structure'), no CTA, no emoji. Hooks with a specific moment from yesterday — true to his observed personal-story openers.

posts.author: Cameron Legge
posts.topicTitle: Your Biggest Customer Might Belong In Your Cheapest Tier
posts.postText:
Working through a packaging exercise with a client right now. One of the biggest retail logos in the world is on their customer list. Top three by ARR.

They use exactly one basic feature of the product.

First instinct from the room was to build them their own premium package. That felt wrong to me. They are already getting full value out of one feature — why would we engineer a tier around them they don't need?

Where they belong is the entry package. The same one a brand new logo would land on.

The real work isn't moving them up the price card. The real work is building the staircase — packages designed to grow into — so that over the next few years, they have a clear path from one basic feature into the more advanced layers of the product.

That's the move. Not 'discount the whale.' Build the staircase that pulls them up.
posts.voiceNotes: Case study snippet with a real (anonymized) customer. Counterintuitive insight delivered in his teacherly register — 'That felt wrong to me' rather than 'Everyone gets this wrong.' Closes with a clean punchy reframe ('Not discount the whale. Build the staircase that pulls them up.') — no signature closer, no hashtags.

posts.author: Yaseen Arshad
posts.topicTitle: The 3 Questions To Decide If Outcome-Based Pricing Works
posts.postText:
Everyone in agentic AI wants to copy Fin AI's closed-ticket pricing.

Most shouldn't.

Before you torch your pricing page, run the three-question gate we use with portfolio teams thinking about going outcome-based.

Three yeses, you're a candidate. Anything less, stay on usage.

The gate, swipe through.
posts.voiceNotes: Stat-as-hook reframe in his pattern — Fin AI as the conventional wisdom, immediate pivot to 'most shouldn't.' Framework callout (the three-question gate) is his signature move. Bullets in the slides are parallel grammatical structure per the voice profile. Closing line is a clean declarative restate, not a CTA.
posts.visualAssetNeeded: 5-slide carousel introducing the three-question gate for outcome-based pricing. Open with a cover slide framing the problem (people are copying Fin AI without checking fit). Three middle slides each unpacking one question. Close with a dark slide delivering the verdict line.

params.title: Should You Move To Outcome-Based Pricing?
params.body: Three questions decide it. Three yeses, you're a candidate. Anything less, stay on usage.

params.title: Can you define one clear outcome?
params.body: Not three. Not 'value delivered.' One outcome a buyer can point to and a system can count.

params.title: Will customers agree with that definition?
params.body: Your version of 'resolved ticket' has to match theirs. If you're still negotiating the definition mid-deal, you're not ready.

params.title: Is the value of that outcome the same across customers?
params.body: A resolved ticket worth $5 to one buyer and $500 to another breaks the model. Outcome pricing needs a tight value band.

params.title: Three yeses, you're a candidate. Anything less, stay on usage.
params.body: Outcome-based pricing isn't the future for everyone. It's the future for a narrow set of vertical AI companies who pass this gate.

posts.author: Yaseen Arshad
posts.topicTitle: Classic SaaS Margins Were A Historical Anomaly
posts.postText:
80-95% gross margins were a once-in-a-generation gift.

Classic SaaS only worked that way because the marginal cost to serve the 10th customer and the 10,000th was effectively the same — basically zero.

AI breaks that math. Every additional call costs compute, and that compute compounds with usage.

Most SaaS founders are running the old playbook on the new economics and haven't done the unit math yet. The bolt-on AI feature looks great on the demo and quietly drags the contribution margin down for every account that actually uses it.

Worth re-running the numbers before the next pricing cycle.
posts.voiceNotes: Industry-observation register, calm and even per his emotional register. Reframes the conventional wisdom (high margins were normal) with a structural reason (zero marginal cost). Lands on an operator takeaway. No emoji, no hype words. Closing line is a quiet operator nudge, not a CTA.
posts.visualAssetNeeded: Single-image stat infographic. Big headline contrast: classic SaaS marginal cost (~$0) vs AI-era marginal cost (compounds with usage). Should read as a 'the math has changed' callout — clean, scannable, save-worthy.

params.title: Classic SaaS Was A Historical Anomaly
params.body: Only possible because marginal cost to serve was ~$0. The 10th customer and the 10,000th cost the same to deliver. AI breaks that assumption — every call costs compute, and compute compounds with usage.

posts.author: Yaseen Arshad
posts.topicTitle: Outcome-Based Pricing Is Mostly A Trap
posts.postText:
Three talks at a recent London RevOps event all pitched outcome-based pricing as the future for everyone.

It isn't.

It works for a narrow slice of vertical AI companies — single, agreeable outcome, tight value band across customers, clean way to measure it. Fin AI, basically.

For horizontal tools? Different users, different use cases, 'outcome' means a different thing in every account. The minute you try to standardize it on a horizontal SKU, you're either underpricing your power users or arguing with your champion mid-renewal.

The vocabulary is racing ahead of the playbook. Worth slowing down before you rewrite the pricing page.
posts.voiceNotes: Behind-the-scenes anchor opener (RevOps event in London) per his hook style. Contrarian-but-constructive — reframes rather than dunks. Uses parallel structure (vertical vs horizontal). Sample-phrasing-style closing line ('The vocabulary is racing ahead of the playbook') matches his understated declarative landings.

posts.author: Yaseen Arshad
posts.topicTitle: Even OpenAI Loses Money On Your Subscription
posts.postText:
OpenAI's own published financials confirm it: at $20/month, they're underwater on heavy users.

Think about that for a second.

The most-funded, most-scaled, most-optimized AI company on the planet — losing money on seat-based pricing for AI.

If they can't make $20/month work, the seat-based SaaS-plus-AI bolt-on most B2B vendors are shipping isn't working either. The losses just live further down the P&L, in cloud bills nobody's attributing back to specific accounts yet.

For my own use, I'm paying $20 and almost certainly costing them 5x that. Multiply that pattern across every power user in your base.

This is the gap most pricing pages haven't closed.
posts.voiceNotes: Pattern-recognition register from his vantage point — he watches AI/GTM patterns and reports back. Sentence rhythm matches: medium declaratives broken up by one-line punches ('Think about that for a second.'). Personal data point ($20 / 5x) grounds the abstract claim. No emoji, no question bait, declarative landing.

posts.author: Yaseen Arshad
posts.topicTitle: Show Me One Good Outcome-Based Pricing Example
posts.postText:
Every conversation about agentic AI right now ends with the same line: 'price on outcomes.'

Great. Name three companies actually doing it well outside of Fin AI.

I've been asking that question for months across podcasts, events, and portfolio calls. Almost nobody can.

If you want to actually pressure-test whether outcome-based pricing fits your business, here's what I'd do:

1. Write down the single outcome you'd charge on. One line. If you need a paragraph, you don't have one.

2. Ask three customers to define that same outcome unprompted. Compare to your definition word-for-word.

3. Calculate the dollar value of that outcome for your top, middle, and bottom decile of accounts. If the spread is more than 3x, the model breaks.

4. Model your COGS per outcome at scale. If the math only works at price points your buyers won't pay, you have a margin problem dressed up as a pricing question.

If you can't pass all four, you don't have an outcome-pricing problem. You have an outcome-definition problem. Solve that first.
posts.voiceNotes: Tactical-playbook structure per the taxonomy — setup line, numbered actionable steps, payoff. Hook is the 'show me three companies' challenge, which is his contrarian-reframe reflex in action. Steps are parallel (each starts with a verb, each has a measurable criterion). Closing line restates the thesis cleanly without a sign-off.

posts.author: Jake Toepel
posts.topicTitle: The Jobs-To-Be-Done Validation Test
posts.postText:
We use a dead-simple test to validate packaging before it ships.

Put the three tiers in front of a real customer. Label the jobs-to-be-done on each. Ask them to point to the one that describes them.

If they pick without hesitation, the packaging works.

If they say "I'm a little of this and a little of that" — the packaging just failed.

Swipe through the test we run on every pricing project at LeanScale Labs.
posts.voiceNotes: Opens with the artifact-led frame ("we use"), builder-pragmatic register. Show-don't-shout. Uses the actual transcript phrasing ("a little of this and a little of that"). Soft practitioner-facing close — names LeanScale Labs naturally, no pitch.
posts.visualAssetNeeded: Five-slide carousel walking through the jobs-to-be-done packaging validation test: setup, the three-tier layout shown to customers, the pass condition, the fail condition, and a closing reminder that ambiguity equals failed packaging.

params.title: The Jobs-To-Be-Done Test
params.body: A one-question check that tells you if your packaging actually works — before you ship it.

params.title: Lay out the three tiers
params.body: Put your full packaging in front of a real customer. Label the jobs-to-be-done on each tier so nothing is hidden.

params.title: Ask one question
params.body: Which tier describes the job you are trying to get done? Stay quiet. Let them point.

params.title: What the answer tells you
items: Hedges between two tiers = boundaries are blurry
Says "a little of both" = packaging failed
Asks clarifying questions = jobs are not legible

params.title: If they cannot pick one without hesitation, the packaging is not done.
params.body: LeanScale Labs — pricing and packaging workstream

params.title: Validate before you launch
params.body: Most packaging fails in front of customers, not in the spreadsheet. Pull three customers this week and run the test.

posts.author: Jake Toepel
posts.topicTitle: Multi-Segment Companies Need Multi-Complexity Pricing
posts.postText:
If you sell to startups and enterprise off the same pricing page, you have a problem.

Startup pricing absorbs almost zero complexity. One number, one tier, self-serve checkout.

Enterprise pricing rewards complexity. Volume curves, seat tiers, usage commits, custom SKUs.

Trying to make one architecture serve both is why your sales team cannot quote either segment cleanly.

The fix is not an elegant unified model. The fix is two architectures running in parallel — and packaging that decides which one a deal lives in.

One image on what that actually looks like.
posts.voiceNotes: Earnest builder voice, no contrarian bait. Frames the problem before the solution. Uses concrete artifacts (volume curves, seat tiers, SKUs) — load-bearing jargon, not decorative. Lands on a practical reframe rather than a hot take.
posts.visualAssetNeeded: Single-slide side-by-side comparison of startup pricing architecture vs. enterprise pricing architecture, showing the different complexity each absorbs and the routing decision that sits between them.

params.title: Multi-segment companies need multi-complexity pricing
items: Startup track: one tier, one number, self-serve
Enterprise track: tiers, commits, seat curves, custom SKUs
Packaging decides which track a deal enters
Sales team quotes against the right model the first time
One unified page is what is breaking your quoting motion

posts.author: Jake Toepel
posts.topicTitle: Pricing Should Be A Product, Not A Tablet From The Mountain
posts.postText:
Pattern we are seeing across the pricing work at LeanScale Labs:

The old rule was "set pricing once, don't touch it for five to seven years."

That rule is dead.

If your product ships new value monthly — new models, new agents, new capabilities — your pricing has to evolve at the same cadence. Otherwise the price stops reflecting what the product actually does.

The teams winning this right now treat pricing like a product team treats a product. Iterations. Releases. Customer feedback loops. A backlog. An owner.

Not a tablet handed down from a Q4 offsite.

If your pricing page hasn't changed in two years and your product has, that gap is showing up in your sales calls.
posts.voiceNotes: Industry-observation register that fits Jake without forcing a contrarian opener. Uses "pattern we are seeing" — his earned vantage from LeanScale Labs. Lands on a concrete diagnostic the reader can run on themselves. No emoji, no hashtag stack.

posts.author: Jake Toepel
posts.topicTitle: Pricing Is An Operations And Product Problem, Not A Sales Problem
posts.postText:
Confusion I keep running into when teams roll out AI pricing:

They treat it as a sales problem. One owner, usually the CRO. Tune for sellability, ship the new SKUs, hope finance can track it.

That model breaks the second AI usage shows up.

When the CRO owns pricing alone, you optimize for what closes — and miss cost-to-serve. When product owns pricing alone, you build elegant feature ladders — and miss value capture.

AI pricing only works when three teams ship in lockstep:

Product ships the metering — what gets counted, at what granularity.

Operations ships the traceability — usage flowing cleanly from product to billing to CRM.

Revenue ships the offer — packaging, discounting rules, quote-to-cash motion.

Stop putting AI pricing in one team's lane. It is an operations and product exercise as much as a pricing one. The teams that get this right are the ones that actually capture the value their AI delivers.
posts.voiceNotes: Soft educational register — clears up a real confusion he is hearing. Uses his "three layers" mental model habit. Plain-spoken, no jargon salad. Land-the-plane closer instead of a CTA. Slightly longer post (~220 words) to match his "explaining a LeanScale Labs experiment" length norm.

posts.author: Jake Toepel
posts.topicTitle: Anthropic's Weekly Pricing Experiments
posts.postText:
I use Claude every day. If you do too, you have probably noticed something.

Anthropic is shipping pricing changes almost weekly.

New credit promos. New plan tweaks. New bundles. Different limits on different tiers. A new add-on appears, an old one quietly gets repackaged.

Most companies would call that chaotic.

It isn't. They are treating pricing as a feature surface — one that ships at the same cadence as the product. And the product ships fast, because the model capabilities ship fast.

The interesting part: this only works because the underlying telemetry is good enough to learn from each iteration. Usage data, conversion data, retention data — all wired into how the next packaging change gets shaped.

That is the new bar for any company building AI-native product.

Not "set pricing carefully and defend it." It is "instrument it like a feature, ship it like a release, learn from it like a product team."

If your pricing only changes when leadership runs an offsite, you are operating on the old playbook against teams running the new one.
posts.voiceNotes: Case-study-snippet about a public example he can credibly comment on as a Claude user. Earnest builder voice — "the interesting part" framing fits his enthusiastic-without-hype register. Closes on a quiet diagnostic instead of a CTA — keeps it from feeling stamped onto the batch.
